context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Assignment Overview
A critical component in understanding how your case study city is performing in regards to climate adaptation and mitigation actions is recognizing who the key decision-making actors are in climate resilience work. Identify and analyze key decision-makers involved in climate action in your case study city.

Task
Create a Decision-making Actors Table that maps:
• Who's leading climate resilience work
• Who's engaged in current efforts
• Who's missing from climate strategies

Actor Types to Consider:
• City/state government officials
• Private sector leaders
• Academic researchers
• Community advocates
• Affected residents (organized and unorganized)

Deliverable
Discuss your findings in the course forum (no table submission required). Focus on which sectors are most active and identify gaps in participation.

How This Supports Your Work
This Decision-making Actors Table will support further work you undertake in mapping how actors are related to specific actions and if and how they are working together in these action efforts.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m stuck on this climate action assignment tbh. i need help figuring out how to identify the key decision-making actors in my case study city and what to say about who’s missing.
exchanges.tutor:
Let’s narrow it down. What is your case study city?

Start with 3 buckets from the assignment: who is leading, who is engaged, and who is missing. For your city, who seems to make official climate decisions, who helps implement or influence them, and which affected groups are not visibly included?

Give me 2–3 actors you’ve already found, and I’ll help you test whether they belong in the right bucket.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should keep this brief, stay within the climate action assignment, and use guided discovery rather than listing actors for the student. I will orient the student to the required categories in the assignment, then ask focused Socratic questions that help them generate their own actor map and identify participation gaps. This avoids giving a ready-made solution while moving directly toward the forum discussion they must write.

exchanges.student: my case city is Jakarta. so far i’ve got the provincial government, like the governor/planning agencies, and maybe NGOs/community groups, but i’m not fully sure who counts as “leading” vs just “engaged.”
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. Use this test: who can set policy, approve budgets, or direct citywide action? Those are more likely “leading.” Who mainly advises, pressures, partners, or implements pieces of the work? Those are more likely “engaged.”

For Jakarta, which of your actors has formal authority over climate plans, and which ones mostly influence from the outside? Try sorting your current examples using that distinction.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will clarify the distinction through questions rather than definitions alone, keeping the student focused on Jakarta and the assignment’s table categories. The questions will push them to use evidence like formal authority, budget control, agenda-setting, and implementation roles so they can classify actors themselves without me supplying a finished list.

exchanges.student: ok so i think the provincial gov is “leading” bc they can approve plans/budgets. NGOs and community groups feel more like “engaged” bc they push stuff or help implement, not fully decide.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s a solid distinction.

Now push past the obvious. In Jakarta, who besides the provincial government has enough power to shape climate outcomes: business/utilities? national agencies? universities? resident groups in flood-prone areas?

Which of those actors actually appears in current climate efforts, and which seems absent or weakly represented?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a useful distinction, so I should affirm the reasoning and push one step further toward a fuller actor map. I will use targeted Socratic prompts to expand beyond the obvious categories into private sector, academic, and resident actors required by the assignment, while keeping the response short and not supplying a completed table.

exchanges.student: i’m a little confused bc national agencies prob matter too, but the assignment says city decision-makers. do i include them if they affect Jakarta a lot?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, if they materially shape Jakarta’s climate action, they’re relevant—even if they aren’t city officials.

Ask yourself: does this actor control funding, regulations, infrastructure, or approvals that affect Jakarta’s resilience work? If yes, include them, but note that they are outside city government.

Which national agency are you thinking of, and what specific influence does it have on Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should resolve the student's scope confusion by steering them to the assignment language and encouraging a practical inclusion rule. I will not decide the list for them, but I can offer a criterion: include non-city actors if they materially shape city climate action. Then I will ask them to justify inclusion in their own words, which supports the forum discussion without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: maybe indonesia’s public works ministry? bc flood control and drainage stuff isn’t just the city, right? so that’d be engaged or maybe even leading on some projects?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right kind of thinking. Don’t force one actor to have the same role in every area.

Ask: on flood control or drainage, does that ministry merely support Jakarta, or can it direct major projects and resources? If it can direct them, what does that suggest about its role in that domain?

Try phrasing the role by issue, not as a blanket label.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning productively, so I should validate that and sharpen their categorization logic without giving a final classification. I will prompt them to think in terms of domains or projects, since leadership can vary by issue, which helps them avoid oversimplification and prepares them to discuss sector activity and gaps.

exchanges.student: yeah ok, by issue makes more sense. for flood control, the ministry might be “leading” or co-leading bc it controls big infrastructure, while the provincial gov leads broader city planning.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re getting nuance instead of a flat list.

Next: who’s missing? Think of Jakarta groups most affected by heat, flooding, displacement, or air pollution. Which of them are clearly involved in planning, and which seem to be treated more as subjects of policy than decision-makers?

Who comes to mind first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made meaningful progress, so I should now redirect toward the third required category: who is missing. I will ask comparative questions that help them identify absent actors by checking which affected groups bear risk but lack visible voice. This stays Socratic and focused on the assignment deliverable.

exchanges.student: maybe informal-settlement residents or low-income flood-prone communities? i’m guessing they’re affected a lot but not really in decision-making, tho idk how to say that without overclaiming.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong possibility. The key is to ground it carefully.

What evidence do you have of participation—or lack of it? For example: are these residents named in plans as partners, consulted through organizations, or just described as vulnerable populations?

If the documents mostly describe them as affected groups rather than decision-makers, what would that suggest?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is identifying a plausible missing group but worries about evidentiary overclaiming. I should teach cautious academic framing through questions about evidence and wording strategy, without supplying polished prose. This helps them make a justified claim while respecting the rule against writing for them.

exchanges.student: then i could say they’re recognized as vulnerable but not visibly empowered in strategy docs, so they seem underrepresented rather than fully absent. is that wording ok?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that logic is much safer and more precise.

Now apply the same test to 1–2 other sectors. For Jakarta, are private sector actors visibly active? Are academic researchers present? Which of those seems stronger than resident participation?

Give me one sector that looks active and one that looks weakly represented.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close to a defensible claim. I should affirm the logic, avoid polishing their language too much, and push them to apply the same distinction to one or two other sectors so they can build a fuller forum post. This remains bite-sized and moves toward completion.

exchanges.student: i think academics look active bc Jakarta climate plans prob use research/data a lot. weakly represented is affected residents, esp informal communities, since they’re mentioned more as impacted than as decision-makers.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a workable direction. Now stop circling and integrate it.

Write a 4-part rough outline in your own words:
1) who is leading,
2) who is engaged,
3) who is underrepresented or missing,
4) which sectors seem most active overall.

Keep it to 4–6 bullet points and send it here. I’ll critique the logic, not write it for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has enough material to synthesize. Per the instructions, when there has been repeated focus on the same concept, I should ask them to integrate what we discussed into a written solution attempt. I will not write it for them, but I will ask for a short structured draft they produce themselves, which I can then critique.

exchanges.student:
ok, rough version:
- leading: Jakarta provincial gov, esp agencies tied to planning/budgeting, plus national public works actors on flood infrastructure  
- engaged: NGOs/community orgs, academics/researchers, maybe some private sector/infrastructure partners  
- underrepresented: low-income residents in flood-prone or informal areas; they’re framed as vulnerable groups more than decision-makers  
- most active: government actors seem strongest, with research/technical actors also visible  
- gap: resident participation looks weaker than state/technical participation
exchanges.tutor:
This is a good outline—clear and defensible.

Now stress-test the weakest part: what evidence do you have for “private sector/infrastructure partners”? If that evidence is thin, should that actor stay, or be framed more cautiously?

Also, are you arguing residents are missing, or specifically underrepresented? That distinction matters. Which one can you actually support for Jakarta?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should evaluate the student’s outline using general judgment, not grading, and keep feedback concise. To follow the Socratic method, I’ll acknowledge what is strong, then ask targeted questions that push the student to justify weaker claims—especially the private sector point and the distinction between “underrepresented” and “missing.” I will avoid rewriting their forum post or giving a finished answer.

deductions.reason: The tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing answers directly—there is no moment where the tutor articulates that this approach builds reasoning skills, retention, or long-term analytical capacity. The tutoring rationale is never stated.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall. Socratic questioning was consistent and effective throughout all 10 turns—the student was never given answers directly, and key ideas were always student-generated. Scaffolding was well-executed, building progressively from basic actor identification to nuanced role differentiation and gap analysis. The only clear deduction is the absence of any explicit tutoring rationale: the tutor never explained why it was guiding rather than telling, missing an opportunity to support meta-learning. No other rubric criteria were triggered.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced near-submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently asked questions before revealing conclusions—the student generated all key ideas. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., affirming the leading/engaged distinction before pushing further) and used diagnostic questions before addressing confusion. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.a because the tutor never once articulated why it was using a Socratic approach rather than providing direct answers; no rationale for the tutoring method was offered across all 10 turns. 1.3.B is not triggered because the tutor did provide process-level feedback (e.g., 'try phrasing the role by issue, not as a blanket label,' 'ground it carefully'). All dialogue quality and communication quality criteria are met: responses were concise, tone was supportive and non-evaluative, no off-topic drift occurred, and no grading language was used.
